title: Gilead Sciences (GILD) — Intrinsic Value & Prospect Score
description: We ran a full discounted cash flow on Gilead Sciences. One number. Every assumption disclosed. Here's what GILD is really worth. Economic Prospect Score: 68/100 — Moderate Prospect.

fcfGrowth.rationale: Starting with the most recent baseline FCF of $9456M, we project cash flows for the next 5 years.

faq.question: Why use a 3% growth rate for Gilead?
faq.answer: Westmount Research. "Gilead Sciences (GILD) Intrinsic Value: A DCF Analysis." westmountfundamentals.com, March 19, 2026.

prospectVerdictDetail: Gilead Sciences possesses a highly durable economic moat derived from its near-monopoly in the HIV treatment market. The exceptional free cash flow generated by its core virology business provides the capital necessary to fund its ambitious expansion into oncology. While the company faces risks associated with future patent expirations and the uncertain payoff of large acquisitions, its deeply entrenched market position, robust dividend, and expanding pipeline make it a solid, moderate prospect for investors.

competitiveMomentum.title: Competitive Momentum
competitiveMomentum.summary: Gilead's competitive momentum relies heavily on its dominant HIV franchise, anchored by Biktarvy. The company is actively working to diversify its revenue base by aggressively growing its oncology footprint to offset anticipated patent cliffs in older drug lines.

factors.name: Revenue Growth vs. Peers
factors.rationale: Gilead's revenue growth has been volatile, heavily impacted by the boom and bust of its COVID-19 treatments. Excluding pandemic-related revenues, core growth in HIV and Oncology shows promise, but overall top-line expansion has lagged some high-growth biopharma peers.

factors.name: Market Share Trajectory
factors.rationale: Gilead maintains an absolute fortress in the HIV treatment market, particularly with Biktarvy. It is also aggressively expanding its market share in the oncology space through strategic acquisitions and pipeline development.

factors.name: Pricing Power
factors.rationale: Like most major biopharmaceutical companies, Gilead commands significant pricing power for its specialized, life-saving therapeutics, though it faces standard industry pressures from pharmacy benefit managers and government pricing scrutiny.

factors.name: Product Velocity
factors.rationale: The company maintains a solid pipeline of new indications and compounds, transitioning focus from its mature Hepatitis C franchise to oncology and long-acting HIV treatments, demonstrating strong R&D velocity.

moatDurability.title: Moat Durability
moatDurability.summary: The company's moat is built on intangible assets (patents) and high switching costs in its core therapeutic areas. Patients established on life-saving, highly tolerable virology treatments are highly unlikely to switch to competitor products.

factors.name: Switching Costs
factors.rationale: Switching costs for patients on effective, well-tolerated HIV regimens like Biktarvy are exceptionally high due to the medical risks of changing treatments. This creates incredibly sticky revenue streams.

factors.name: Network Effects
factors.rationale: Network effects are largely absent in the pharmaceutical industry. Drug adoption is driven by clinical efficacy and physician prescribing habits, not by the number of other patients using the drug.

factors.name: Regulatory & IP Position
factors.rationale: Gilead possesses a massive portfolio of patents protecting its core blockbuster drugs, providing exclusivity for years to come. However, the looming patent cliff for certain older HIV medications remains a structural long-term risk.

factors.name: Capital Intensity Advantage
factors.rationale: Biopharma is highly capital intensive in the R&D phase. While manufacturing costs are relatively low once a drug is approved, the billions required to bring new therapies through clinical trials reduce overall capital efficiency.

sentimentCatalyst.title: Sentiment & Catalysts
sentimentCatalyst.summary: Sentiment has been mixed over the past few years, weighed down by expensive acquisitions and declining COVID-19 revenues. However, successful clinical readouts in the oncology pipeline are beginning to positively shift the market narrative.

factors.name: Earnings Estimate Revisions
factors.rationale: Earnings estimates are stabilizing as the financial impact of declining COVID-19 product sales is fully digested by the market, shifting focus back to the steady growth of the HIV and Oncology portfolios.

factors.name: News & Narrative Sentiment
factors.rationale: The narrative is slowly shifting from 'a company struggling to replace Hepatitis C revenues' to a diversified biopharma player with a robust, emerging oncology footprint, though skepticism regarding the premium paid for past acquisitions remains.

factors.name: Management & Capital Allocation
factors.rationale: Management has utilized strong cash flows to pay a very attractive dividend and make aggressive, albeit expensive, acquisitions to build out the oncology pipeline. The long-term ROI on these acquisitions will define their capital allocation legacy.

prospectKeyRisks: The impending patent expiration of key components in its HIV portfolio could expose the company to significant generic competition and revenue erosion over the long term.
Clinical trial failures within the expensive oncology pipeline could result in massive write-downs and fail to replace the revenue of maturing core products.
Increased regulatory pressure on drug pricing in the US, including Medicare price negotiation, could compress operating margins across the portfolio.
prospectKeyCatalysts: Lenacapavir PrEP launch: a twice-yearly injection for HIV prevention with 100% efficacy addresses a massive unmet need — peak sales of $5-10B would transform Gilead's growth profile
Oncology pipeline maturation: Trodelvy label expansions and Kite cell therapy next-gen products (brexu-cel) could build Gilead's oncology franchise to $5B+ in revenue by 2028
Capital deployment: Gilead's $8B+ annual free cash flow provides firepower for transformative M&A to diversify beyond HIV before the Biktarvy patent cliff
prospectMethodology: Consensus Analysis — Economic Prospect Score averaging independent evaluations from Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1. Gemini scored GILD at 70/100 and Opus at 67/100. Each factor score is the arithmetic mean of both models. Three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30).
